walkthrough.title: Download the Companion App
walkthrough.text: Before we do anything else, let's make sure everyone has access to the Mansions of Madness companion app. This game is completely app-driven, so we need the app running on a tablet, phone, or computer. You can find it on iOS, Android, Mac, or Steam. One device for the whole group is fine, but make sure it is in a spot where everyone can see and interact with it.

walkthrough.title: Select a Scenario
walkthrough.text: Open the app and tap on a scenario to play. For your first game, the app will recommend a starter scenario. Each scenario has a different story, map layout, and estimated play time. Read the scenario description aloud so everyone knows the theme we are diving into.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Investigators
walkthrough.text: Now each player picks an investigator. Find the investigator cards and look at their five stats: Strength, Agility, Observation, Lore, and Influence. Each investigator also has a Health value and a Sanity value. Try to pick a balanced team. We want someone strong for combat, someone observant for searching, and someone with high Lore for spells and horror checks. Select your investigators in the app as well.

walkthrough.title: Gather Investigator Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their investigator's card and matching plastic miniature. Place your investigator card in front of you where you can see your stats. We will track damage and horror with tokens placed on these cards throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Token Pools
walkthrough.text: Separate out the damage tokens, horror tokens, clue tokens, exploration tokens, search tokens, and door tokens. Keep them in piles within easy reach of all players. We will be drawing from these pools frequently as the app tells us to place or remove them.

walkthrough.title: Organize the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Now let's sort the card decks. Separate common items, unique items, spells, conditions, and insanity cards into their own face-down piles. We do not shuffle these. When the app tells us to draw a specific card, we will find it by name from the appropriate deck. Keep them organized so we can locate cards quickly during play.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Monster Figures
walkthrough.text: Set the monster miniatures and monster tokens off to the side where we can reach them. We will not need them immediately, but when the app spawns a monster, we need to place the matching figure on the map quickly. The app will tell us exactly which monster appears and where to put it.

walkthrough.title: Ready the Dice
walkthrough.text: Find the custom eight-sided dice and place them in the center of the table. These are used for all skill tests during the game. A result of five or higher counts as one success. We will be rolling these frequently, so keep them accessible to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Build the Starting Map
walkthrough.text: Now the app will show us the starting map layout. Find the map tiles it displays and assemble them on the table matching the orientation shown in the app. These large double-sided tiles represent rooms and corridors. Make sure the correct side is face up and the tiles connect properly.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Tokens
walkthrough.text: The app will indicate where to place exploration tokens, search tokens, and door tokens on the map. Follow its instructions carefully. Exploration tokens mark spots we can investigate to reveal new rooms. Search tokens mark places where we might find useful items. Door tokens show doorways that may need to be opened.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Items
walkthrough.text: The app may assign starting items to certain investigators. When it does, find those cards from the common or unique item decks and give them to the appropriate player. Place your items face up near your investigator card so you can reference them during your turn.

walkthrough.title: Place Investigator Figures
walkthrough.text: Place all investigator miniatures on the starting space indicated by the app. In most scenarios, everyone begins in the same room. This is our base of operations as we begin our investigation.

walkthrough.title: Read the Scenario Introduction
walkthrough.text: The app will now present the scenario's narrative introduction. Read it aloud or let the app narrate. This sets the stage for our mystery and gives us initial clues about what we need to accomplish. Pay attention to the story details because they often hint at what we should prioritize.

walkthrough.title: Begin the Investigation
walkthrough.text: We are all set up and ready to play. The app will indicate when to begin the first investigation phase. Remember, we are a team of investigators working together against whatever horrors lurk in this mansion. Let's coordinate our actions and watch each other's backs. Time to investigate.

summary.title: Begin the Investigation
bullets: App signals start of the first investigation phase
Cooperative game — coordinate actions as a team

walkthrough.title: The Two Phases
walkthrough.text: Every round in Mansions of Madness has two phases. First comes the Investigation Phase, where all investigators take their turns. Then comes the Mythos Phase, which the app controls. During the Investigation Phase, we decide what to do. During the Mythos Phase, the mansion fights back. This cycle repeats until we either solve the mystery or get overwhelmed.

walkthrough.title: Investigation Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: During the Investigation Phase, each investigator takes a turn in any order we choose as a group. On your turn, you get two actions. You can pick from several action types, and you can take the same action twice only if it is the move action. Once every investigator has taken their turn, we press the Mythos Phase button in the app.

walkthrough.title: Action: Move
walkthrough.text: Each distinct area on a map tile counts as one space. You can move through open doorways freely, but locked or barricaded doors block your path until they are dealt with. Move is the only action you can take twice on the same turn, letting you cover up to four spaces if needed.

walkthrough.title: Action: Explore
walkthrough.text: Tap the exploration token in the app, and it will tell you what happens. This usually reveals new map tiles to place on the table, along with new tokens and sometimes narrative events. Exploring is how we expand the map and advance the story.

walkthrough.title: Action: Search
walkthrough.text: The app will tell you what you find, which is often a common item, unique item, or clue. Items go into your investigator's inventory and can be used on future turns. Searching is our main way to gear up with weapons and tools.

walkthrough.title: Action: Interact
walkthrough.text: This could mean reading a journal, talking to a non-player character, or examining a strange artifact. The app handles the narrative result. Some interactions trigger puzzles that appear directly in the app for you to solve.

walkthrough.title: Action: Attack
walkthrough.text: Choose a weapon from your inventory. The weapon tells you which stat to test, typically Strength for melee weapons or Agility for ranged weapons. Roll dice equal to that stat and count your successes. Then enter your result in the app, and it resolves the damage.

walkthrough.title: Skill Tests
walkthrough.text: Many actions require skill tests. The app will ask you to test one of your five stats: Strength, Agility, Observation, Lore, or Influence. Roll a number of eight-sided dice equal to your stat value. Every die showing a five or higher counts as one success. The app tells you how many successes you need and what happens based on your result.

walkthrough.title: Action: Trade
walkthrough.text: You can exchange any number of items, including weapons, tools, and spells. This is a great way to get the right equipment to the right investigator. The person with the highest Strength should carry the melee weapons, for example.

walkthrough.title: Action: Use Component
walkthrough.text: Some items and spells have abilities you can activate as an action. Check your item cards for abilities that say you can use them as an action. Spells are especially powerful but risky. When you cast a spell, the app typically requires a Lore test, and failure can have painful side effects on top of wasting your action.

walkthrough.title: The Mythos Phase
walkthrough.text: After every investigator has taken their turn, we press the Mythos Phase button in the app. The app now takes control. It activates monsters, making them move toward investigators and attack. It triggers environmental events like collapsing floors or spreading fire. It advances the hidden doom timer that creeps toward our defeat. We cannot control anything during the Mythos Phase. We just respond to what the app throws at us.

walkthrough.title: Monster Attacks
walkthrough.text: When the app activates a monster, it tells us how the monster moves and which investigator it attacks. The app specifies what kind of test to make to defend. A failed defense means taking damage tokens, horror tokens, or both. Some monsters deal physical damage, others deal mental horror, and the scariest ones do both at once.

walkthrough.title: Damage and Horror
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the two ways investigators get hurt. Damage tokens reduce your Health, and horror tokens reduce your Sanity. Most tokens are placed face down and simply count against your pool. Some are flipped face up, which means they also apply a specific condition like Stunned or Restrained. When your face-down damage plus face-up damage equals your Health, you are in serious trouble.

walkthrough.title: Wounded and Eliminated
walkthrough.text: When an investigator's damage equals their Health, they become wounded. Flip your investigator card to the wounded side, which has reduced stats. If a wounded investigator takes damage equal to their Health again, they are eliminated from the game. Protect each other. Once an investigator goes down, the team has fewer actions per round to work with.

walkthrough.title: Insanity
walkthrough.text: When an investigator's horror equals their Sanity, they go insane. Draw an insanity card and read it secretly. This card gives you a new hidden condition that changes how you must behave. Some insanity cards are harmless quirks, but others can turn you against the team with a secret personal objective. You stay in the game but your priorities may shift dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Horror Checks
walkthrough.text: Certain events force investigators to make horror checks, typically when encountering a terrifying monster or witnessing something disturbing. The app will prompt you to test your Lore stat. Failures result in horror tokens being placed on your investigator card. These checks can happen during both the Investigation Phase and the Mythos Phase.

walkthrough.title: Puzzles
walkthrough.text: Some interactions trigger in-app puzzles. These are mini-games like sliding tile puzzles, lock combinations, or rune matching. You get a limited number of moves per attempt based on your relevant stat. If you do not solve it in one try, you can spend another action on a future turn to continue where you left off. Puzzles are a unique and fun part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Map Expansion
walkthrough.text: The map grows as we explore. When the app tells us to place new tiles, we find the matching tile, orient it as shown, and connect it to the existing map. New rooms come with their own exploration tokens, search tokens, and sometimes monsters. The full map is never visible at the start. Discovery is a core part of the experience.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Scenario
walkthrough.text: Each scenario has a unique objective managed entirely by the app. We win by completing that objective, whether it is solving the mystery, performing a ritual, escaping the mansion, or defeating a boss monster. The app will announce when we have won. Our choices during the game affect the narrative path, so different groups may experience different story outcomes.

walkthrough.title: Losing the Scenario
walkthrough.text: We lose if all investigators are eliminated, if the app's hidden doom timer expires, or if certain story events go catastrophically wrong. There is no point scoring in Mansions of Madness. Each scenario is a self-contained story that ends in either victory or defeat. If we lose, we can always try again. The randomized elements mean the experience will be different next time.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Mansion
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first few rounds of a scenario with three example investigators. We have a fighter with high Strength, a seeker with high Observation, and a mystic with high Lore. Follow along with the moves even if your team is different. Let's step inside.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Room
walkthrough.text: We start in the entrance hall. The app has placed two exploration tokens and one search token in nearby rooms. Our first priority is to fan out and cover as much ground as possible. The doom timer is always ticking in the background, so every round we waste standing still is a round closer to defeat.

walkthrough.title: The Fighter's Turn: Move and Explore
walkthrough.text: Our fighter goes first. With two actions, we spend the first action to move two spaces toward the nearest exploration token. Then we spend the second action to explore. The app reveals a new hallway with a locked door and a search token. We place the new tiles and tokens as shown. Great start. We have expanded the map and opened up new possibilities.

walkthrough.title: The Seeker's Turn: Move and Search
walkthrough.text: Our seeker moves one space to reach the room with the search token, then spends the second action to search. The app tells us we found a flashlight, which is a common item that gives a bonus to Observation tests. Perfect for our seeker. Items go to the investigator who searched for them. We place the flashlight card next to the seeker's investigator card.

walkthrough.title: The Mystic's Turn: Move and Interact
walkthrough.text: Our mystic moves toward an interesting object the app highlighted, then uses the interact action. The app presents a journal entry that gives us a clue about the mystery. Pay attention to these story moments. They often hint at what we need to do next or warn us about dangers ahead. The mystic's high Lore makes them ideal for these narrative interactions.

walkthrough.title: The First Mythos Phase
walkthrough.text: All three investigators have taken their turns. Now we press the Mythos Phase button. The app describes an eerie sound from deeper in the mansion and advances the doom timer by one step. No monsters yet, but the tension is building. In early rounds, the Mythos Phase is usually mild. It gets much worse as the game progresses.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Not Exploring Fast Enough
walkthrough.text: Here is a critical mistake new players make. Let's say our fighter decides to search for items instead of exploring. They find a knife, which is nice, but they did not advance the story at all. Meanwhile, the doom timer ticked forward during the Mythos Phase. In Mansions of Madness, exploration is almost always more important than searching early on. The story will not wait for us to gear up.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: A Monster Appears
walkthrough.text: We start round two. Our fighter explores another token, and this time the app spawns a monster in the newly revealed room. We grab the matching miniature and place it on the map. The app tells us what kind of creature it is and where exactly it goes. Do not panic. We have time to prepare before the Mythos Phase forces it to attack.

walkthrough.title: The Fighter Attacks
walkthrough.text: Our fighter has a weapon and is in the same space as the monster. Let's attack. We select our weapon, which requires a Strength test. Our fighter has a Strength of four, so we roll four dice. We get two successes, which is the number of dice showing five or higher. We enter two successes into the app, and it tells us we dealt damage to the monster.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Everyone Fights the Monster
walkthrough.text: Here is another common mistake. Our seeker and mystic both rush to help fight the monster, spending all their actions on movement and combat. But the seeker has low Strength and misses entirely, wasting both actions. Meanwhile, there were two unexplored rooms that could have advanced the story. Not every investigator needs to fight. Send your fighter to handle monsters while others keep investigating.

walkthrough.title: Splitting Up Wisely
walkthrough.text: The better play is to split up. Our fighter handles the monster alone while the seeker explores a new room and the mystic searches for a needed item. We cover three objectives in one round instead of having all three investigators pile onto one monster. In a cooperative game with a ticking timer, efficiency is everything.

walkthrough.title: A Horror Check
walkthrough.text: During the Mythos Phase, the app tells us that the monster lets out a terrifying shriek. All investigators in its space must make a horror check. Our fighter tests Lore, which is only two dice. We roll and get zero successes. The app assigns two horror tokens to our fighter. Horror accumulates fast on investigators with low Lore. This is why team composition matters.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Sanity
walkthrough.text: New players often focus entirely on Health and forget about Sanity. Our fighter has taken two horror tokens and only has five Sanity total. If horror keeps piling up, our fighter will go insane and draw an insanity card that could sabotage the team. Monitor both tracks carefully. Sometimes retreating from a scary monster is smarter than standing and fighting if your Sanity is low.

walkthrough.title: Solving a Puzzle
walkthrough.text: Our mystic interacts with a strange device and the app presents a sliding tile puzzle. The mystic has a Lore of five, which means five moves to try to solve it. We rearrange the tiles in the app, but do not solve it in one attempt. That is okay. On a future turn, we can spend another action to continue from where we left off with another five moves. Puzzles are rarely solved in one try.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Wrong Investigator on the Puzzle
walkthrough.text: Puzzle attempts are based on your stat value. If our fighter with Lore two tried this puzzle, they would only get two moves per attempt. It would take them three or four attempts to solve what the mystic can do in two. Always send the investigator with the highest relevant stat to handle puzzles and tests. Matching stats to tasks is one of the most important strategic decisions.

walkthrough.title: Trading Items
walkthrough.text: Our seeker found a fire axe during a search, but the seeker has low Strength. The fighter is in the same room and would use it much better. We spend one action to trade the axe from the seeker to the fighter. One action well spent. Always get weapons to the investigators who can use them most effectively.

walkthrough.title: The Doom Timer Pressure
walkthrough.text: Several rounds have passed and the Mythos Phase events are getting worse. More monsters are spawning, environmental hazards are spreading, and the app's narrative is growing more urgent. This is by design. The doom timer creates mounting pressure that forces us to stay focused on the objective. We cannot afford to wander aimlessly or get bogged down fighting every monster.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Trying to Kill Everything
walkthrough.text: Our team has spent the last two rounds fighting every monster on the map. We have taken heavy damage and horror in the process, and we have not advanced the story at all. This is a trap. Not every monster needs to die. Sometimes the best strategy is to evade a monster by moving away and focusing on exploration and puzzle solving. Fight only when a monster blocks a critical path.

walkthrough.title: Coordinating as a Team
walkthrough.text: Let's take a moment to discuss strategy as a group. We look at the map and plan our next moves together. The fighter will hold off the monster near the library. The seeker will explore the last unexplored room in the east wing. The mystic will return to the puzzle and try to finish solving it. This kind of coordinated planning is what separates winning teams from losing ones.

walkthrough.title: Approaching the Objective
walkthrough.text: After several rounds of exploration, searching, and puzzle solving, the app reveals the scenario's main objective. Maybe we need to perform a ritual, find a hidden passage, or confront a boss monster. Whatever it is, we now shift our entire team toward completing it. Every action from this point forward should directly serve the objective.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now experienced the core loop of Mansions of Madness. You have explored rooms, searched for items, fought a monster, solved a puzzle, and weathered the Mythos Phase. You have seen the five most common beginner mistakes: not exploring fast enough, everyone piling onto one monster, ignoring Sanity, using the wrong investigator for tasks, and trying to kill everything instead of focusing on the objective. Avoid these traps and you will give your team the best chance of surviving the mystery.

walkthrough.title: Before You Enter the Mansion
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first real scenario, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on team coordination, resource management, and pacing.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to actions, conditions, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck, investigators!

Every action should directly serve the goal from this point

bullets: Core loop experienced: explore, search, fight, puzzle, Mythos
5 beginner mistakes: slow exploring, group combat, ignoring Sanity, wrong stats, killing everything
Avoid these traps for the best chance at survival

summary.title: Before You Enter the Mansion
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for team coordination
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for actions and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Explore First, Fight Later
walkthrough.text: The number one priority in Mansions of Madness is advancing the story through exploration. The doom timer does not care how many monsters you have killed. It only cares about time passing. In the early rounds, send investigators toward every exploration token on the map before worrying about combat. New rooms reveal clues, items, and objectives. Every exploration token you ignore is a potential game-winning clue you are leaving on the table while the clock ticks down.

walkthrough.title: Match Stats to Tasks
walkthrough.text: Every skill test in the game uses one of five stats, and each investigator excels at different ones. Send your high-Strength investigator to fight monsters, your high-Observation investigator to search rooms, your high-Lore investigator to cast spells and solve puzzles, and your high-Influence investigator to interact with characters. When you match the right person to the right task, you roll more dice and get more successes. When you mismatch, you waste actions failing tests that someone else would have passed easily.

walkthrough.title: Protect Your Sanity
walkthrough.text: New players consistently underestimate horror damage. Insanity is often more dangerous to the team than being wounded, because insanity cards can secretly turn an investigator against the group. Watch your Sanity track closely. If an investigator is getting low, pull them away from monsters and horror-inducing events. High-Lore investigators are naturally more resistant to horror because they roll more dice on horror checks. Use them as your front-line defense against psychological threats.

walkthrough.title: Don't Fight Every Monster
walkthrough.text: Not every monster needs to die. Some monsters are damage sponges that will eat up three or four actions to kill while the doom timer marches forward. Before engaging a monster, ask yourself whether it is blocking a critical path or threatening a vulnerable investigator. If the answer is no, consider walking around it instead. Use the move action to step past monsters when possible and keep pushing toward the objective.

walkthrough.title: Trade Items Early
walkthrough.text: Items found during searches go to the investigator who searched, but that person is not always the best user for that item. A fire axe in the hands of a Lore-focused mystic is mostly useless, but in the fighter's hands it becomes devastating. Spend one action to trade items as soon as you can. The earlier you get the right equipment to the right investigator, the more efficient every subsequent turn becomes.

walkthrough.title: Coordinate Your Actions
walkthrough.text: Since investigators can take their turns in any order during the Investigation Phase, use that flexibility to your advantage. Discuss your plan as a group before anyone acts. If the fighter plans to kill a monster blocking a doorway, have the seeker go second so they can walk through the cleared path and explore the room beyond. Think of your team as one coordinated unit, not three or four individuals doing their own thing.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Doom Timer
walkthrough.text: The Mythos Phase gets worse every round. Early rounds might just have atmospheric flavor text, but later rounds bring multiple monster spawns, environmental damage, and accelerating doom events. This means the first few rounds are your most valuable. Explore aggressively, search for key items, and set up your team's positioning early. The longer the game goes, the harder it gets. Front-load your progress and you give yourself the best chance of winning before the mansion overwhelms you.

walkthrough.title: Read the Story Carefully
walkthrough.text: Mansions of Madness is a narrative game, and the app's story text often contains important clues. When the app describes a room, mentions a character, or presents a choice, read it carefully and discuss it with your team. Sometimes the difference between winning and losing is catching a hint in the narrative about where to go next or what item you need. Do not skip through the story text to get back to the action. The story is the action.

summary.title: Explore First, Fight Later
bullets: Exploration advances the story — combat doesn't affect the doom timer
Prioritize exploration tokens over fighting in early rounds
Every unexplored token is a potential game-winning clue

summary.title: Match Stats to Tasks
bullets: High Strength → fight; High Observation → search; High Lore → spells/puzzles
Right stat = more dice = more successes
Mismatching wastes actions on failed tests

summary.title: Protect Your Sanity
bullets: Insanity can turn investigators against the team — worse than wounds
Pull low-Sanity investigators away from horror sources
High-Lore investigators resist horror better

summary.title: Don't Fight Every Monster
bullets: Some monsters are damage sponges — not worth the action cost
Ask: does this monster block a critical path or threaten someone?
If no, walk around it and focus on the objective

summary.title: Trade Items Early
bullets: Items go to the searcher, not necessarily the best user
1 trade action gets the right weapon to the right investigator
Early trading = more efficient turns for the rest of the game

summary.title: Coordinate Your Actions
bullets: Investigators act in any order — plan together before acting
Sequence turns: fighter clears path → seeker explores behind
Think as one coordinated unit, not solo players

summary.title: Manage the Doom Timer
bullets: Mythos Phase escalates each round — early rounds are most valuable
Front-load exploration and item gathering
The longer the game goes, the harder it gets

summary.title: Read the Story Carefully
bullets: Narrative text contains important clues and hints
Discuss story choices with the team before deciding
Don't skip the story — it IS the game

walkthrough.title: Team Composition Planning
walkthrough.text: Before you even start the app, think carefully about your investigator lineup. The ideal team covers all five stats with at least one investigator strong in each. You want at least one high-Strength fighter to handle combat, one high-Observation searcher to find items efficiently, and one high-Lore mystic to handle spells, puzzles, and horror checks. For three-player games, consider investigators who cover multiple roles with well-rounded stats rather than extreme specialists.

walkthrough.title: Action Economy Optimization
walkthrough.text: Every action in Mansions of Madness is precious because the doom timer never stops. The most efficient players combine movement with purpose. Never take a move action without a clear plan for your second action. If you move to a room but cannot explore, search, or interact there, you have wasted half your turn. Before moving, scan the map and identify the spot where your second action generates the most value.

walkthrough.title: Monster Avoidance Tactics
walkthrough.text: Advanced players know that monsters are often obstacles to manage rather than enemies to destroy. When a monster spawns, assess its position relative to your objective. If it is not blocking a critical path, leave it alone and route around it. Some monsters pursue investigators during the Mythos Phase, so plan your positioning to keep them chasing you through empty rooms rather than through rooms where other investigators are working.

walkthrough.title: Spell Management
walkthrough.text: Spells are the highest-risk, highest-reward tools in the game. A well-timed attack spell can eliminate a dangerous monster in one action, but a failed casting can deal horror to your mystic when they can least afford it. Never cast a spell when your Sanity is dangerously low unless you have no other option. Save offensive spells for moments when you absolutely must clear a path, and use utility spells to support the team when the risk-reward ratio is favorable.

walkthrough.title: Puzzle Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Puzzles reward methodical thinking and stat matching. Always send your highest-stat investigator for the relevant test. If a puzzle requires Lore, your Lore-five mystic gets five moves per attempt while a Lore-two fighter only gets two. That difference can mean solving the puzzle in two attempts versus five, saving three entire actions. When attempting a puzzle, focus on the most constrained pieces first. For sliding puzzles, work from the edges inward.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Mythos Escalation
walkthrough.text: The Mythos Phase follows a predictable escalation pattern. Early rounds feature atmosphere and minor events. Mid-game introduces regular monster spawns and environmental hazards. Late-game hits with multiple threats per round and accelerated doom progression. Understanding this curve helps you plan your pacing. Push hard in rounds one through three when the Mythos is mild. By round five or six, you should be working on the main objective, not still exploring.

walkthrough.title: Positioning for the Mythos Phase
walkthrough.text: Before pressing the Mythos Phase button, think about where your investigators are standing. Monsters activate during Mythos and will move toward the nearest investigator. If your wounded fighter is standing next to a powerful monster at the end of the Investigation Phase, that fighter is about to take more damage. Consider ending your turn in a defensible position. Sometimes spending your last action to move away from a monster is smarter than getting one more search in.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Decision Making
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds of a scenario, every decision is magnified. The question is no longer about whether to explore or search but rather about committing your remaining actions entirely to the objective. If you need to solve one more puzzle to win, throw your entire team at supporting the puzzle solver. If you need to reach a specific room, clear the path with everything you have. Late-game Mansions of Madness is about calculated all-in pushes toward victory, not cautious resource management.

summary.title: Team Composition Planning
bullets: Cover all 5 stats with at least 1 strong investigator each
Need: fighter (Strength), searcher (Observation), mystic (Lore)
3-player games: prefer well-rounded over extreme specialists

summary.title: Action Economy Optimization
bullets: Never move without a plan for your second action
Scan the map before moving — identify highest-value second action
Wasted actions = wasted rounds against the doom timer

summary.title: Monster Avoidance Tactics
bullets: Assess monster position vs. objective — fight only if blocking
Route around non-threatening monsters
Position to lead pursuing monsters through empty rooms

summary.title: Spell Management
bullets: High risk / high reward — failed spells deal horror to caster
Never cast at low Sanity unless no other option
Save offensive spells for critical path-clearing moments

summary.title: Puzzle Efficiency
bullets: Always send highest-stat investigator for the relevant test
Stat 5 vs. Stat 2 = solving in 2 attempts vs. 5 (saves 3 actions)
Sliding puzzles: work edges first, then center

summary.title: Reading the Mythos Escalation
bullets: Early rounds: atmosphere; Mid-game: regular spawns; Late-game: overwhelming
Push hard in rounds 1-3 while Mythos is mild
By round 5-6, be working on the main objective

summary.title: Positioning for the Mythos Phase
bullets: Before pressing Mythos button, check investigator positions
Monsters activate and move toward nearest investigator
Sometimes retreating before Mythos is smarter than one more search

summary.title: Late-Game Decision Making
bullets: Final rounds: commit everything to the objective
Throw entire team at supporting the win condition
Late-game = calculated all-in pushes, not cautious play

entries.definition: The first phase of each round where all investigators take their turns. Players choose the turn order. Each investigator gets two actions per turn. Ends when all investigators have acted.

The second phase of each round, controlled entirely by the companion app. Monsters activate, environmental events trigger, and the doom timer advances. Players press the Mythos button to begin this phase.
Action that lets an investigator move up to two spaces. The only action that can be taken twice in a single turn, allowing up to four spaces of movement. Blocked by locked or barricaded doors.
Action that interacts with an exploration token in the investigator's space. The app reveals new map tiles, tokens, narrative events, and sometimes monster spawns. Primary way to advance the story.
Action that interacts with a search token in the investigator's space. The app provides items (common or unique) or clues. Main way to acquire weapons, tools, and equipment.
Action that engages with objects, NPCs, or story elements highlighted by the app. May trigger narrative events, skill tests, or in-app puzzles. Often requires specific stats.

Action to attack a monster in the investigator's space using a weapon. Requires a skill test based on the weapon type (Strength for melee, Agility for ranged). Successes are entered into the app to resolve damage.
Action to exchange any number of items with another investigator in the same space. Costs one action for the trading investigator. Used to get the right equipment to the right person.
Action to activate an ability on an item card or spell card. Some items have special abilities beyond their passive bonuses. Spells require a Lore test and may have negative side effects on failure.
Action to push a monster out of your space into an adjacent space. Useful when you need to clear a room without fighting. Requires a Strength test.
Stat used for melee combat, pushing monsters, and physical feats. Investigators with high Strength are your front-line fighters. Tested when attacking with melee weapons.
Stat used for ranged combat, dodging, and physical agility tests. Tested when attacking with ranged weapons like firearms. Also used for evading certain hazards.
Stat used for searching, spotting clues, and awareness tests. High-Observation investigators find items and clues more reliably. Critical for efficient searching.

Stat used for spellcasting, horror checks, puzzle solving, and occult knowledge tests. The most versatile defensive stat because horror checks use Lore. High-Lore investigators resist insanity better.
Stat used for social interactions, persuasion, and willpower tests. Used when interacting with NPCs or making diplomatic choices in the narrative. Less commonly tested than other stats.

Core dice mechanic. Roll a number of eight-sided dice equal to your stat value. Each die showing five or higher counts as one success. The app specifies the required number of successes and the outcome.
A result of five or higher on an eight-sided die during a skill test. Multiple successes are counted and reported to the app, which determines the outcome based on the total.
A Lore skill test triggered by encountering monsters or disturbing events. Failures result in horror tokens placed on the investigator's card. Can occur during both Investigation and Mythos phases.

An in-app mini-game triggered by certain interactions. Types include sliding tile puzzles, lock combinations, and rune matching. The number of moves per attempt equals the investigator's relevant stat value. Can be resumed on future turns.

A hidden timer managed by the app that advances during each Mythos Phase. When it reaches its endpoint, the investigators lose. Creates mounting pressure to complete the objective quickly. Not visible to players.
An investigator's physical resilience. Reduced by damage tokens. When damage equals Health, the investigator becomes wounded. If wounded again to zero, they are eliminated from the game.
An investigator's mental resilience. Reduced by horror tokens. When horror equals Sanity, the investigator goes insane and draws a secret insanity card that may change their behavior or objectives.
Token placed on an investigator's card representing physical harm. Face-down tokens simply count against Health. Face-up tokens also apply specific conditions like Stunned or Restrained.

Token placed on an investigator's card representing mental trauma. Face-down tokens count against Sanity. Face-up tokens apply psychological conditions. Horror accumulates from failed horror checks and Mythos events.

Status when an investigator's damage equals their Health. The investigator card flips to the wounded side, which has reduced stat values. A wounded investigator who reaches zero Health again is eliminated.

A secret card drawn when an investigator's horror equals their Sanity. Contains a hidden condition that changes the investigator's behavior, goals, or win condition. Some insanity cards turn the investigator against the team.

Basic equipment cards found through searching. Includes weapons like knives and firearms, tools like flashlights and lockpicks, and supplies like first aid kits. Drawn by name from the common item deck when the app instructs.

Powerful one-of-a-kind equipment cards. Stronger than common items with special abilities. Found through searching, story events, or scenario-specific triggers. Drawn by name from the unique item deck.

Magical ability cards that provide powerful effects but carry risk. Casting requires a Lore test. Success grants the spell's benefit. Failure may deal horror to the caster or cause other negative effects. Types include attack, protection, and utility spells.

Status effect cards applied to investigators. Conditions include Stunned (lose an action), Restrained (cannot move), Poisoned (take damage each round), and others. Applied by face-up damage or horror tokens and monster attacks.

Token placed on the map marking a point of interest that can be explored. Using the Explore action on this token triggers the app to reveal new map tiles, story events, and additional tokens. Advances the scenario narrative.
Token placed on the map marking a searchable location. Using the Search action on this token triggers the app to provide items, clues, or information. Primary source of equipment and gear.

Token representing a doorway between spaces. May be open, locked, barricaded, or secret. Locked doors require interaction or specific items to open. Barricaded doors may need Strength tests to break through.
Large double-sided modular board pieces representing rooms and corridors. Assembled according to the app's instructions as the map expands through exploration. Each tile contains one or more spaces.

A distinct area within a map tile. Movement is measured in spaces. Each room section or corridor segment counts as one space. Investigators and monsters occupy spaces, and many actions require being in the same space as a target.

The required digital application that manages the game. Controls the scenario narrative, map reveals, monster AI, puzzles, doom timer, and all story branching. Available on iOS, Android, Mac, and Steam.

A self-contained story with unique objectives, map layouts, and narrative events. Each scenario has a different mystery to solve. The base game includes multiple scenarios, and expansions add more. Scenarios have variable elements for replayability.
A player character with unique stats, Health, Sanity, and sometimes special abilities. Each player controls one investigator. The base game includes eight investigators to choose from.
